author: y4rr
content:
Result: refactored ferrotorch-core/src/einops.rs to dispatch on device for the common cases. Three GPU-aware fast paths now bypass the CPU round-trip:

1. **rearrange identity-permutation** (e.g. `'b c h w -> b (c h w)'`, `'b (c h) w -> b c h w'`): pure flatten/unflatten now runs as a zero-copy view_reshape on any device. New tests verify the output shares storage with the input via raw pointer comparison.

2. **rearrange general permute** (contiguous input): now uses view_reshape → permute → contiguous → view_reshape via existing tensor ops, instead of data_vec() → manual loops → cpu(...) → to(device). Currently still hits a CPU round-trip inside contiguous() for strided CUDA tensors — see #496.

3. **reduce axis-aligned** (e.g. `'b c h w -> b c'`, `'b c -> c'`): when the kept axes are in the same relative order on both sides AND the reduced axes form a contiguous run in the left elementary order, the reduction is expressed as view_reshape([outer, reduced, inner]) → sum_dim/cummax/cummin → reshape. Sum is direct sum_dim. Mean is sum_dim → multiply by scalar 1/N (mean_dim isn't GPU-aware, so we compose it from sum_dim). Max/Min use cummax/cummin (GPU-native scans) followed by narrow to the last index — the running extreme ends with the global extreme along the reduced dim.

Repeat() and the general non-aligned reduce / non-contiguous-reduced-axes case still take the legacy CPU index-walk fallback. These need #496 (gpu_strided_copy) before they can become GPU-native — repeat needs broadcast→materialize, and the non-aligned reduce needs permute→contiguous before sum_dim.

Tests: added 7 new unit tests in einops::tests covering identity-perm rearrange (verifies zero-copy via pointer eq), pure unflatten (same), and axis-aligned reduce for Sum/Mean/Max/Min/full-pool. All 29 einops tests pass; full ferrotorch-core lib (859 tests) passes with no regressions and zero new warnings.

Filed follow-up #496 for the general gpu_strided_copy primitive that would close the remaining gap.
